note: AI-generated explanations cached client-side. Commit this file to data/<exam>/explanations.json to share across devices.

1.text:
Option B is correct because the symptom—tasks repeatedly stopping and starting because ALB health checks fail right after a new task definition is deployed—typically means the new container needs more time to initialize than the health check allows. The ECS service health check grace period tells the service to ignore ALB health check results for a configured number of seconds after a task starts, giving the application time to boot and pass checks before ECS considers it unhealthy and replaces it. Increasing this grace period stops the churn while the new image warms up.

Option A is incorrect because if the security group did not allow ALB traffic, the original steady-state tasks would also have failed health checks; the deployment was healthy before the image change, so connectivity is not the cause. Option C is incorrect because the minimum healthy percent controls how many tasks stay running during a deployment; it does not stop newly started tasks from being killed by failing health checks. Option D is incorrect because decreasing the health check interval makes checks run more frequently, which would cause tasks to be marked unhealthy faster, worsening the problem rather than resolving it.

2.text:
Options B, E, and F are correct because offering self-service customizations through AWS Control Tour uses the Account Factory Customizations (blueprint) and Customizations for AWS Control Tower (CfCT) pattern. Option F provides the actual building blocks: a CloudFormation template defining the resources for each customization. Option B creates the AWSControlTowerBlueprintAccess IAM role with a trust policy for the management account's AWSControlTowerAdmin role and the AWSServiceCatalogAdminFullAccess policy, which is the exact prerequisite role Control Tower requires to provision blueprints through Service Catalog into enrolled accounts. Option E deploys the CfCT framework, the AWS-supported solution that lets accounts request and apply customizations via the landing zone pipeline.

Option A is incorrect because enabling trusted access for CloudFormation StackSets with Organizations supports a stack set deployment model, not the Control Tower blueprint customization workflow this scenario requires. Option C is incorrect because manually creating a Service Catalog product per template is a partial, manual step that bypasses the integrated blueprint/CfCT mechanism. Option D is incorrect because building standalone CloudFormation stack sets with automatic deployment to OUs is a separate deployment approach that does not deliver customizations "requested through AWS Control Tower" as required.

3.text:
Option A is correct because AWS OpsWorks Stacks uses Chef and exposes a Configure lifecycle event that fires on every instance in a layer whenever an instance comes online or goes offline. This is exactly the trigger needed: each membership change re-runs the recipe across the cluster, repopulating /etc/cluster/nodes.config with the current layer members and restarting the service. OpsWorks provides the current online instances to the recipe automatically, so the cluster reconfigures itself on every node add or removal with no custom orchestration.

Option B is incorrect because CodeDeploy deployments are triggered manually or by pipeline and require committing an updated file and deploying on each change; it does not automatically react to membership changes the way the Configure event does, and tag-based discovery of new instances would be manual. Option C is incorrect because polling an S3 file via crontab introduces latency, still requires manually editing and uploading the file on every change, and is a fragile, eventually-consistent design. Option D is incorrect because a user data script runs only once at instance boot, so it cannot update existing nodes when members are added or removed later, and querying a security group for membership is not a reliable cluster discovery mechanism.

4.text:
Options C and D are correct because the message loss occurred while the downstream Lambda/RDS path was unavailable, so messages must be durably buffered instead of dropped. Option D subscribes an SQS queue to the SNS topic and has Lambda poll the queue; SQS persistently retains messages until they are successfully processed, so a database outage no longer loses notifications—Lambda simply retries when the database is back. Option C configures an SQS dead-letter queue for the SNS subscription so that any notifications SNS cannot successfully deliver are captured for later inspection and reprocessing rather than discarded, adding a second layer of durability.

Option A is incorrect because swapping RDS for DynamoDB changes the data store but does not address the root cause: messages are still lost if the delivery target is unavailable. Option B is incorrect because configuring an SQS queue as a Lambda destination only routes the results of asynchronous Lambda invocations after they run; it does not buffer the incoming SNS notifications that were lost before processing. Option E is incorrect because replacing SNS with EventBridge to invoke Lambda still delivers events to the function synchronously without a durable buffer for outages, so messages can still be lost during downstream failures.

5.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Service Catalog lets you publish only pre-approved CloudFormation templates as products, restricting business teams to launching just those approved resources. A launch constraint associates an IAM role with the product so users provision resources through that role without needing broad permissions themselves, enforcing least privilege while still limiting them to approved templates. Pairing this with AWS Config rules gives the security team automated, continuous detection of resources that drift from their expected configuration.

Option A is incorrect because CloudFormation drift detection is not automatic or continuous—it must be initiated and does not natively provide ongoing monitoring, and a service role alone does not restrict users to only pre-approved templates. Option B has the same weakness: a CloudFormation service role does not constrain users to approved templates the way Service Catalog does. Option D is incorrect because a template constraint only limits the parameter values users can supply, not which templates they launch, and EventBridge notifications do not provide configuration drift evaluation; AWS Config is the service designed for ongoing drift and compliance detection.

6.text:
Option C is correct because AWS CodeDeploy blue/green deployments for EC2/Auto Scaling provision a brand-new replacement fleet with the same capacity as the original (the blue fleet), keep the original fleet running and unchanged while the green fleet is built and validated, then shift the ALB's traffic to the new fleet once it is healthy. CodeDeploy provides a setting to terminate the original instances after a configurable waiting period, which can be set to one hour—satisfying every stated requirement directly.

Option A is incorrect because a CloudFormation ALB retention policy does not create a second equal-capacity fleet or orchestrate traffic transition, and manually updating Route 53 is not the described automated cutover. Option B is incorrect because Elastic Beanstalk blue/green requires swapping environment CNAMEs and there is no application version lifecycle policy that terminates an environment one hour after the swap; that lifecycle policy manages stored application versions, not environments. Option D is incorrect because Elastic Beanstalk immutable deployments replace instances within a single environment rather than standing up a separate persistent fleet with a timed transition, and a CloudFormation DeletionPolicy cannot be set to a one-hour delay—DeletionPolicy is not time-based.

7.text:
Option C is correct because CloudWatch Logs Insights is purpose-built for ad hoc, retrospective analysis of log data. The engineer can write a query that filters log events to the specific username and uses an aggregation function such as count() over the past 7 days, then run it directly against the existing log group. This requires no pre-configuration before the event occurred and returns the exact count needed for the root cause analysis.

Option A is incorrect because a metric filter only counts events that arrive after the filter is created; it cannot retroactively count logins that already happened over the previous 7 days. Option B is incorrect for the same retroactive reason, and a CloudWatch Logs subscription streams log data to another destination for real-time processing rather than counting historical events. Option D is incorrect because a CloudWatch dashboard number widget visualizes existing metrics; it cannot apply a filter pattern directly against raw log group data to count historical logins, so it relies on a metric that was never created in advance.

8.text:
Options A and C are correct because AWS Firewall Manager centrally enforces WAF web ACL associations across all accounts in an organization. Option A delegates Firewall Manager administration to a dedicated security account, which is the required prerequisite for managing firewall policies organization-wide from a single account. Option C creates a Firewall Manager security policy that automatically attaches WAF web ACLs to in-scope resources such as ALBs and API Gateway APIs, including newly created ones, which directly prevents the future violations the audit found.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service; it neither enforces nor attaches WAF web ACLs. Option D is incorrect because GuardDuty has no policy type that attaches WAF web ACLs to resources—that is not a GuardDuty capability. Option E is incorrect because AWS Config managed rules can detect and report noncompliant resources and trigger remediation, but there is no AWS Config managed rule that natively attaches WAF web ACLs across an organization; enforcing and auto-associating web ACLs is the role of Firewall Manager.

9.text:
Options A and D are correct because deploying an RPM with CodeDeploy to an Auto Scaling group requires the CodeDeploy agent on the instances and a CodeDeploy application targeting the group. Option A bakes the CodeDeploy agent into a new version of the common AMI and grants the EC2 instance IAM role the permissions CodeDeploy needs, ensuring every instance the Auto Scaling group launches can be managed by CodeDeploy. Option D creates a CodeDeploy application with an in-place deployment type targeting the Auto Scaling group and wires a CodeDeploy action into the existing pipeline's deployment stage, so new instances that scale out automatically receive the latest deployed revision.

Option B is incorrect because an AppSpec file defines deployment lifecycle hooks; it is part of the application revision, not a means of installing the agent, and it does not grant access to CodeDeploy. Option C is incorrect because building a new AMI with EC2 Image Builder on every deployment is unnecessary operational overhead when CodeDeploy can install the RPM directly. Option E is incorrect because targeting individual EC2 instances rather than the Auto Scaling group means instances launched later by scaling events would not receive deployments.

10.text:
Option D is correct because the requirement is to guarantee that every EBS volume always carries the Backup_Frequency tag, defaulting to weekly. Using CloudTrail with an EventBridge rule that reacts to both CreateVolume and ModifyVolume events ensures tagging is applied not only when a volume is created but also if it is later modified in a way that could affect its state, and a Systems Manager Automation runbook applies the default weekly tag. Covering both event types closes the gap that a create-only solution would leave.

Option A is incorrect because the custom AWS Config rule scope is overly broad (all EC2 resources) and AWS Config evaluates periodically rather than at creation time, leaving volumes untagged until the next evaluation. Option B has the same delayed, periodic evaluation weakness compared to event-driven tagging, so volumes can exist untagged in the interim. Option C is incorrect because reacting only to CreateVolume events misses the ModifyVolume case, so a volume changed after creation would not be guaranteed to retain or receive the tag, making it less complete than Option D.

11.text:
Option C is correct because the cold-start latency is caused by the 8–10 second initialization, and provisioned concurrency keeps function instances initialized and ready, eliminating cold starts. Because traffic varies dramatically—10x at midday and 10% near end of day—configuring Application Auto Scaling on provisioned concurrency with a minimum of 1 and a maximum of 100 scales the warm capacity up and down with demand, keeping latency low at all times of day without over-provisioning during quiet periods.

Option A is incorrect because a fixed provisioned concurrency of 1 cannot absorb the 10x midday spike, and deleting the DAX cluster removes in-memory caching that helps the data load, harming performance. Option B is incorrect because reserved concurrency of 0 effectively disables the function by preventing any invocations—the opposite of the goal. Option D is incorrect because reserved concurrency only caps how many concurrent executions a function can use; it does not pre-initialize environments to remove cold starts, and Application Auto Scaling does not apply to API Gateway in this way, so cold-start latency would persist.

12.text:
Option A is correct because CodePipeline does not have a native action that simply blocks on a CloudWatch alarm state, so the health gate must be implemented as an invokable workflow. An AWS Step Functions workflow can read the Route 53/CloudWatch alarm state and deliberately fail (exit with an error) when the alarm is in the ALARM state. Inserting a new pipeline stage between each Region's deployment stage that invokes this Step Functions workflow causes the pipeline to halt before proceeding to the next Region whenever the just-deployed application is unhealthy, exactly meeting the requirement.

Option B is incorrect because it restructures the deployment to CodeDeploy and removes the CloudFormation actions, which is a major rework that does not match the existing CloudFormation-based pipeline and addresses rollback rather than a between-Region health gate. Option C is incorrect because there is no "CloudWatch alarm action" within a CodePipeline stage that checks an alarm and exits with an error—pipeline stages run actions, not alarm evaluations. Option D is incorrect for the same reason and additionally misuses the CloudWatch agent, which reports metrics but does not feed Route 53 health checks or provide a native pipeline gating action.

13.text:
Options B and D are correct because least-privilege, scoped access to a Secrets Manager secret requires controlling the encryption key. Option B creates a KMS customer managed key whose key policy trusts Secrets Manager (via the kms:ViaService condition) and grants decrypt only to the Lambda function's execution role, then reconfigures the secret to use this key—so only that role can decrypt the secret value. Option D ensures the Lambda execution role has KMS permissions scoped at the resource level to that specific key, completing the tightly bounded access path required for least privilege.

Option A is incorrect because the AWS managed default KMS key for Secrets Manager cannot have its key policy edited, so you cannot restrict decryption to a single role on it. Option C is incorrect because granting the account root principal decrypt access is far broader than least privilege, since any IAM principal in the account could be granted access. Option E is incorrect because removing all KMS permissions from the execution role would prevent the Lambda function from decrypting the secret at all, breaking its ability to retrieve the API keys.

14.text:
Options C and D are correct because a stack stuck in UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED means CloudFormation could not roll back one or more resources, often because a resource is in a state CloudFormation cannot reconcile. The fix is to manually adjust the offending resources so they match what the stack expects (Option D)—for example, fixing or deleting a resource that blocked the rollback—and then issue ContinueUpdateRollback (Option C) from the console or CLI, which tells CloudFormation to retry and complete the rollback now that the underlying problem is resolved.

Option A is incorrect because broadening the CloudFormation role with AWSCloudFormationFullAccess does not address resource-level failures and is rarely the cause of a rollback failure. Option B is incorrect because drift detection only reports differences between actual and expected configuration; it cannot recover resources or advance a failed rollback. Option E is incorrect because you cannot perform a normal stack update while the stack is in UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED—CloudFormation requires the rollback to complete (via ContinueUpdateRollback) before any further update is allowed.

15.text:
Option B is correct because the engineer needs a metric per API operation, broken down by response code and application version, where the version is parsed from the user-agent header inside the Lambda function. Writing each request's operation name, response code, and version as a structured log line to CloudWatch Logs captures the data, and a CloudWatch Logs Insights query can aggregate and populate CloudWatch metrics using response code and application version as dimensions—supporting the many concurrent versions in use without predefining a fixed set.

Option A is a common distractor because metric filters can emit metrics with dimensions, but metric filter dimensions must come from known, fixed values and do not scale well to the large, changing set of application versions parsed at runtime, making it brittle here. Option C is incorrect because ALB access logs capture request metadata at the load balancer and cannot include custom application version data extracted inside Lambda, and ALB does not relay Lambda response metadata into those logs. Option D is incorrect because X-Ray is a distributed tracing service for latency and dependency analysis; X-Ray insights do not produce per-operation, per-version, per-response-code business metrics in the manner required.

16.text:
Option D is correct because centralizing CloudWatch Logs from many accounts into one S3 bucket is achieved with a CloudWatch Logs destination backed by a Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream in the dedicated account, which writes log data to the S3 bucket. Subscription filters on the source log groups stream their events to that destination. To cover future log groups automatically, an EventBridge rule that reacts to CreateLogGroup events invokes a Lambda function calling PutSubscriptionFilter, ensuring newly created log groups are enrolled without manual action—satisfying both existing and future coverage.

Option A is incorrect because Organizations backup policies do not back up CloudWatch Logs log groups to S3; they govern AWS Backup-supported resources. Options B and C are incorrect because AWS Backup does not support CloudWatch Logs log groups as a protected resource type and cannot use an S3 bucket as a backup vault in this manner, so assigning log groups to a backup plan is not a valid mechanism for exporting log data. Subscription filters with a Firehose-backed Logs destination is the established pattern for cross-account log centralization to S3.

17.text:
Option B is correct because the most operationally efficient, fully CloudFormation-defined way to domain-join Auto Scaling instances is to use the AWS-provided AWS-JoinDirectoryServiceDomain Systems Manager Automation runbook. Defining an AWS::SSM::Association in the template that targets instances by the tags the launch template propagates means every new instance the Auto Scaling group launches is automatically joined to the AWS Managed Microsoft AD domain. Attaching the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore and AmazonSSMDirectoryServiceAccess managed policies gives the instances exactly the permissions Systems Manager needs to manage and join them—no custom scripts or credential handling.

Option A is incorrect because an AWS::SSM::Document plus a launch template SSMAssociation property is a more bespoke approach than using the existing managed join runbook and adds maintenance overhead for a document AWS already provides. Options C and D are incorrect because they store domain administrator credentials in Secrets Manager and attach the overly broad SecretsManagerReadWrite policy, violating least privilege and adding credential management complexity; the AmazonSSMDirectoryServiceAccess policy already enables joining without exposing admin credentials. Option C additionally misuses a runbook intended for approval-based manual provisioning.

18.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon GuardDuty is the AWS threat detection service that analyzes VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, and CloudTrail to detect reconnaissance activity, including a finding type specifically for EC2 port scans (Recon:EC2/Portscan). Once GuardDuty is enabled, creating a CloudWatch alarm (or EventBridge rule) on those port scan findings and connecting it to the SNS topic delivers automatic notification whenever a port scan against the EC2 instances is detected—exactly the behavior the company lacked.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector assesses software vulnerabilities and network reachability (which ports are reachable), not active port scanning behavior occurring in real time, so it would not detect the scan event. Option C is incorrect for the same reason: Inspector reports CVEs and open-port exposure, not the act of an attacker scanning. Option D is incorrect because CloudTrail records AWS API management activity, not in-VPC network traffic such as port scans; a Lambda function parsing CloudTrail logs could not detect packet-level scanning of EC2 instances.

19.text:
Option D is correct because the two requirements—vulnerability notification and a login audit trail—map cleanly to two services. Amazon Inspector continuously scans EC2 instances for software vulnerabilities and surfaces new findings, satisfying the requirement to be notified of newly discovered vulnerabilities. Installing the Amazon CloudWatch agent captures operating system logs, including login activity, and ships them to CloudWatch Logs, providing the searchable, retained audit trail of logins on the instances.

Option A is incorrect because the Kinesis Agent forwards log data but is not the standard way to collect OS login logs, and Systems Manager is not the vulnerability scanner of record—Inspector is. Option B is incorrect because login activity at the OS level does not appear in the CloudTrail console; CloudTrail records AWS API calls, not in-instance logins, and the SSM Agent does not by itself capture and centralize system logs as the requirement needs. Option C is incorrect because CloudWatch does not detect software vulnerabilities and AWS Config tracks resource configuration changes rather than collecting OS-level system logs.

20.text:
Options A and C are correct because a deployment whose events are all in the Skipped status, with no code reaching any instance, indicates CodeDeploy could never communicate with or recognize the target instances. Option A is valid because if the instances have no network path to the CodeDeploy/S3 service endpoints (no internet or NAT/VPC endpoint route), the CodeDeploy agent cannot poll for the deployment, so all lifecycle events are skipped. Option C is valid because if the target EC2 instances were not properly registered with CodeDeploy (the agent not running or instances not matching the deployment group), CodeDeploy has no healthy instances to act on and skips the events.

Option B is incorrect because an IAM user lacking permission to trigger the deployment would cause the deployment to fail to start or be denied, not produce a started deployment with Skipped events. Option D is partly related but, in this scenario, missing instance profile permissions typically surface as deployment or agent errors rather than a clean Skipped state across all events, and the more direct causes are connectivity and registration. Option E is incorrect because a missing appspec.yml causes a deployment lifecycle failure on the instance, not skipped events with no deployment occurring at all.

21.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Config organizational rules (also called organization config rules) deploy a Config rule across all accounts in the organization, including accounts added in the future, providing centralized, continuous compliance evaluation—here checking whether EBS volumes are encrypted and marking unencrypted ones as noncompliant. Adding a service control policy that prevents stopping or deleting AWS Config ensures the compliance check is always present and cannot be disabled in member accounts, satisfying the requirement that the check is automatically deployed and always enforced.

Option A is incorrect because there is no "AWS Inspector rule" for EBS encryption, and relying on an account-creation script pointing to an S3-hosted template is fragile and not continuously enforced. Option C is incorrect because an SCP can prevent launching instances with unencrypted volumes but does not mark existing volumes as noncompliant, and Athena analysis of CloudTrail is reactive reporting, not a continuous compliance check. Option D is incorrect because a custom CodePipeline/Lambda solution that lists volumes and publishes a report is operationally heavy, not continuously evaluated, and lacks the always-present guarantee that an organization Config rule with a protective SCP provides.

22.text:
Option C is correct because pipeline activity and approval events are emitted as CodePipeline state-change events that Amazon EventBridge can match. Creating an EventBridge rule filtering on CodePipeline Pipeline Execution State Change captures these events near real time. Publishing to an SNS topic and subscribing a Lambda function that formats and posts to the chat tool's webhook URL bridges the gap, because the custom chat tool expects an HTTP webhook payload that SNS cannot natively format—Lambda transforms the event into the webhook's required structure and delivers it immediately.

Option A is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs subscriptions operate on log data, not pipeline state-change events, and an SNS topic cannot directly post to an arbitrary chat webhook in the expected format. Option B is incorrect because invoking a Lambda function from CloudTrail events is indirect and delayed, and EventBridge is the proper, near-real-time source for CodePipeline state changes. Option D is incorrect because embedding webhook calls in the pipeline code is brittle, hard to maintain, and tightly couples notification logic to the application, whereas an event-driven EventBridge/Lambda approach is decoupled and reliable.

23.text:
Option A is correct because a custom AWS Config rule backed by Lambda requires AWS Config to be allowed to invoke that function. When the Lambda function fails to run on evaluation, the cause is almost always that the function's resource-based policy does not grant the config.amazonaws.com service principal permission to invoke it. Adding an invoke permission (lambda:InvokeFunction) to the function's resource policy for AWS Config lets the service trigger the function on each evaluation, resolving the failure.

Option B is incorrect because the SNS topic policy governs whether EventBridge can publish notifications after a noncompliant result; it has nothing to do with whether the Config rule's Lambda function can be invoked. Option C is incorrect because the execution role determines what the function can do once running (its outbound permissions), not whether AWS Config is permitted to invoke it; the failure to even run points to a missing invoke permission, not execution-role scope. Option D is incorrect because granting AWS Config access to ECR API actions does not address Lambda invocation, and the function reads ECR policy data through its own execution role, not via repository policies.

24.text:
Option B is correct because the requirement is to ensure newly launched Auto Scaling instances always reach and maintain the correct operating system configuration. A Systems Manager State Manager association applies and continuously enforces a desired-state configuration document on targeted instances, and by targeting instances with a tag query (using the tags the Auto Scaling group applies on launch), every new instance is automatically brought into the correct configuration and kept compliant over time without manual intervention.

Option A is incorrect because Run Command with Systems Manager Compliance focused on patch compliance addresses patching state, not general operating system configuration enforcement on launch. Option C is incorrect because a maintenance window that runs Run Command daily applies configuration only on a schedule, so newly launched instances could run misconfigured until the next daily window, rather than being configured immediately on launch. Option D is incorrect because Patch Manager patch baselines and patch groups manage OS patching, not the broader operating system configuration the requirement describes, and invoking patching via Run Command does not continuously enforce the desired configuration state the way a State Manager association does.

25.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Systems Manager Inventory natively collects metadata about installed applications across all managed instances, and AWS Config can consume that inventory data through managed rules that evaluate configuration state continuously. Pairing Inventory with AWS Config rules creates a fully managed, declarative detection loop that flags prohibited applications automatically whenever inventory changes, with no custom code to maintain. This is the most operationally efficient pattern because both services are serverless and integrate directly.

Option A works but adds unnecessary operational overhead by routing data through resource data sync to S3 and then maintaining custom Lambda code to parse findings, which you must patch, test, and debug. Option C is incorrect because CloudTrail records API activity, not the contents of Systems Manager Inventory; you cannot reliably enumerate installed applications by filtering CloudTrail events. Option D is the most operationally intensive choice because it requires authoring and distributing a custom inventory script to every instance, forwarding output to CloudWatch Logs, and tuning metric filter patterns and alarms manually.

26.text:
Option C is correct because the company wants deployment frequency and mean time to recovery, which are DORA-style metrics that must be persisted and correlated over time rather than read as point-in-time CloudWatch metrics. An EventBridge rule that invokes a Lambda function after every successful and every failed pipeline run captures the exact event timestamps needed to compute lead time and recovery time, and writing those records to DynamoDB gives durable, queryable storage that QuickSight can visualize directly. Event-driven invocation is also more accurate and lower effort than polling.

Options A and B are incorrect because CloudWatch custom metrics are time-series numeric data points and are not well suited to calculating durations such as time between releases or time to recover; building those derived metrics requires extra computation. Option D is incorrect because invoking the Lambda function every 5 minutes on a schedule (like option A) means polling pipeline state rather than reacting to actual run events, which adds configuration effort and risks missing or duplicating runs that complete between polls.

27.text:
Options B and D are correct because together they build an automated image pipeline with vulnerability gating. Option B uses an Amazon EventBridge rule to invoke the CodePipeline when a new Dockerfile version is committed to CodeCommit; EventBridge (not S3 events) is the supported, native mechanism for triggering pipelines on CodeCommit changes. Option D enables Amazon ECR enhanced scanning, which is powered by Amazon Inspector and detects both operating system and programming language package vulnerabilities; an EventBridge rule that matches ECR image scan events and filters on finding-severity-counts greater than 0 for CRITICAL or HIGH severity then notifies the SNS topic.

Option A is incorrect because CodeCommit pushes are surfaced through EventBridge, not Amazon S3 events. Option C is incorrect on two counts: ECR basic scanning only covers operating system packages, not programming language packages, and image scan findings are emitted by ECR/Inspector, not by Amazon GuardDuty, so the EventBridge pattern would never match. Option E is incorrect because scanning a Dockerfile statically does not detect OS or language package CVEs in the built image, and it omits the EventBridge-driven notification flow required for HIGH and CRITICAL findings.

28.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Control Tower Account Factory Customization (AFC) lets you associate a blueprint that defines a baseline configuration with accounts at provisioning time. When Account Factory provisions each environment account and the CI/CD account, the AFC blueprint automatically applies the baseline as part of account creation, and because the accounts are created inside Control Tower, the landing zone's controls (guardrails) are enforced on every account. This delivers both the baseline and guardrail enforcement in a single managed workflow with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because, while Account Factory enrolls accounts under Control Tower governance, layering CloudFormation StackSets on top is a separate, manually maintained step for the baseline, adding overhead that AFC blueprints eliminate. Options C and D are incorrect because provisioning accounts directly through AWS Organizations bypasses Control Tower Account Factory, so the new accounts are not automatically enrolled in the landing zone and do not receive Control Tower controls. Option C compounds this by requiring custom Lambda code and assumed roles, and option D still relies on separately managed StackSets without guaranteed Control Tower enrollment.

29.text:
Option C is correct because the s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only AWS Config managed rule detects S3 buckets whose policies do not deny non-HTTPS (unencrypted in transit) requests, and deploying it as a conformance pack across the organization gives consistent, organization-wide evaluation. Pairing the rule with a Systems Manager Automation runbook that adds a bucket policy statement denying access when aws:SecureTransport is false remediates each noncompliant existing bucket automatically, enforcing TLS for all data in transit exactly as required.

Options A and B are incorrect because AWS Network Firewall inspects VPC network traffic and blocking port 80 does not govern how clients connect to the regional S3 endpoint over the public internet; S3 access control is enforced through bucket policies and the aws:SecureTransport condition, not by a network firewall. Option D is incorrect because the s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption-aws-kms-key-id condition key relates to encryption at rest with SSE-KMS, not encryption in transit; using it would enforce KMS encryption of stored objects rather than requiring TLS connections, so it does not meet the stated requirement.

30.text:
Option B is correct because CloudFormation cannot delete an S3 bucket that still contains objects, and a custom resource backed by a Lambda function is the standard pattern to handle this. By modifying the custom resource's Lambda code so that when RequestType is Delete it recursively empties the bucket (deleting all objects and versions) before signaling success, the stack deletes cleanly. Because the fix lives in the reusable custom resource, it also protects future versions of the website stack from the same failure.

Option A is incorrect because an active website configuration does not block bucket deletion; a non-empty bucket does. Option C is incorrect because DeletionPolicy applies to resources whose deletion CloudFormation manages directly and there is no RemoveOnDeletion value; custom resources are cleaned up by their own Lambda logic, not by a deletion policy. Option D is incorrect because DeletionPolicy accepts values such as Delete, Retain, or Snapshot, not Empty, so CloudFormation would reject the template and it would never empty the bucket.

31.text:
Options A and B are correct because the secure, least-privilege way to give EC2 instances access to S3 is an IAM role delivered through an instance profile. Option A creates an IAM role scoped to only the required source and destination S3 actions and attaches it to an instance profile. Option B then references that instance profile in the Auto Scaling launch template, so every instance the group launches automatically assumes the role and receives automatically rotated temporary credentials from the instance metadata service, with no long-lived secrets to distribute across hundreds of instances.

Option C is incorrect because creating an IAM user and generating a static secret key and token uses long-lived credentials, which violates the temporary-credentials requirement and is an anti-pattern for EC2. Option D is incorrect because trust anchors and profiles belong to IAM Roles Anywhere, which is designed for workloads running outside AWS that present X.509 certificates, not for EC2 instances that should simply use an instance profile. Option E is incorrect because embedding a secret key and token in launch template user data hardcodes long-lived credentials and exposes them in plaintext.

32.text:
Option B is correct because the requirement is to stop instances only when the metric is low for at least 3 hours within a 12-hour window, evaluated hourly, while still running when data is missing. Setting Datapoints to Alarm to 3 out of 12 with a 1-hour period implements the M-out-of-N evaluation: 3 breaching hourly datapoints inside the 12-hour window trigger the ALARM state. Treating missing data as notBreaching ensures the instance keeps running when NetworkPacketsIn has no data, and the native EC2 action stops the instance directly with no extra automation.

Option A is incorrect because treating missing data as breaching would stop instances during data gaps, violating the requirement, and a Systems Manager action is unnecessary overhead when a native EC2 stop action exists. Option C is incorrect on both the 9-out-of-12 threshold, which would require 9 low hours rather than 3, and on treating missing data as breaching. Option D is incorrect because 9 out of 12 again misrepresents the 3-hour requirement, and stopping via a Systems Manager action adds complexity over the built-in EC2 action.

33.text:
Option C is correct because including ReportBatchItemFailures in the event source mapping's FunctionResponseTypes enables partial batch responses. The Lambda function returns the identifiers of only the messages that failed, so successfully processed messages are deleted from the SQS standard queue immediately rather than being returned to the queue and redelivered with the rest of the batch. This both maximizes Lambda scalability, because you can keep large batches, and prevents already-processed messages from being handled again.

Option A is incorrect because shrinking the batch window only changes how long Lambda waits to fill a batch and does not stop reprocessing of successful messages when one item in a batch fails. Option B technically prevents reprocessing by isolating each message in its own invocation, but a batch size of 1 severely limits throughput and works against maximizing scalability. Option D is incorrect because the visibility timeout governs how long a message is hidden after being received; tuning it does not prevent successfully processed messages from reappearing when a sibling message in the batch fails.

34.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Config provides the managed rule ec2-imdsv2-check, which evaluates whether each EC2 instance is configured to require IMDSv2 (HttpTokens set to required). When the rule reports a noncompliant instance, an AWS Config remediation action backed by a Systems Manager Automation runbook can terminate that instance automatically. This is a fully managed detect-and-remediate pattern that continuously enforces the control without custom code.

Option B is incorrect because a permissions boundary using a RunInstances condition only prevents launching new instances that allow IMDSv1; it does nothing to terminate existing or already-running instances that permit IMDSv1, and the condition key would be ec2:MetadataHttpTokens at launch rather than an ongoing control. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Inspector focuses on software vulnerabilities and network reachability, not on auditing the IMDS version configuration, so it is not the right detection mechanism. Option D is incorrect because reacting only to the instance launch event misses instances that change configuration later and requires building and maintaining custom Lambda inspection and termination logic, which is more overhead than the managed AWS Config rule.

35.text:
Option A is correct because it uses fully managed, native cross-Region replication for both data stores. Converting the DynamoDB tables to global tables with the secondary Region added gives active-active, multi-Region replication that propagates writes within seconds using built-in managed infrastructure. Two-way (bidirectional) S3 replication keeps the buckets synchronized in both directions so data written in either Region persists across both. Both mechanisms are AWS-managed, requiring no custom code, which makes this the most operationally efficient option.

Option B is incorrect because S3 Batch Operations copy jobs are a one-time or scheduled bulk action, not continuous replication, so they do not propagate new objects immediately or automatically. Options C and D are incorrect because they replace managed DynamoDB global tables with self-built DynamoDB Streams plus custom Lambda functions that copy records between Regions; this adds significant operational burden, risks replication loops and conflicts, and is far less efficient than global tables. Option D additionally relies on the non-continuous S3 Batch Operations approach for the buckets.

36.text:
Option C is correct because Organizations account creation must be performed from the management account, so the function in the new dedicated account needs to assume a role that lives in the management account. Creating an IAM role in the management account with organizations:CreateAccount permission and a trust policy that allows the Lambda service principal lets the function assume that role and create accounts. Granting the Lambda execution role permission to assume the management-account role, and scoping the assumed role to only account-creation actions, satisfies the least-privilege, create-accounts-only requirement before deployment.

Option A is incorrect because it configures the trust policy to be assumed by the Lambda execution role rather than the Lambda service principal, which is not how cross-account role assumption for a Lambda function is correctly established. Option B is incorrect because Organizations delegated administration does not allow a member account to call CreateAccount; account creation remains restricted to the management account, and there is no delegation policy that grants this. Option D is incorrect because it introduces AWS Control Tower unnecessarily and its CreateManagedAccount API and delegation model do not satisfy the requirement to call the Organizations CreateAccount API directly.

37.text:
Option B is correct because for an Amazon ECS blue/green deployment defined in CloudFormation, you use the AWS::CodeDeployBlueGreen transform together with the AWS::CodeDeploy::BlueGreen hook to declare the blue/green deployment within the template. Specifying the CodeDeployDefault.ECSLinear10PercentEvery1Minutes deployment configuration shifts exactly 10% of traffic to the new task set every minute until all traffic is moved, and because a blue/green model keeps the original task set running until the shift completes, the application remains highly available.

Option A is incorrect because an AppSpec file alone does not provide the CloudFormation-managed blue/green orchestration; CloudFormation requires the transform and hook, and the typo in the configuration name does not map to a real deployment configuration. Options C and D are incorrect because the ECSCanary10Percent5Minutes configuration shifts traffic in two large steps rather than 10% every minute, which does not match the required linear, per-minute shifting. Option D also misspells the hook (AWS::CodeDepioy), and option C again relies only on an AppSpec file instead of the required transform and hook.

38.text:
Options A, B, and F are correct because each removes a throughput bottleneck in the Kinesis-to-Lambda path. Option F increases the number of shards, raising the stream's overall read and write capacity so more records can be processed in parallel. Option A registers the consumer Lambda function as an enhanced fan-out consumer, giving it a dedicated 2 MB/sec per-shard throughput pipe with lower propagation latency instead of sharing standard throughput. Option B increases the ParallelizationFactor in the event source mapping, allowing multiple concurrent Lambda invocations to process records from a single shard simultaneously, which directly increases processing concurrency.

Option C is incorrect because reserved concurrency caps the maximum concurrent executions for the function; it can actually limit parallelism rather than reduce latency. Option D is incorrect because increasing the batch size makes each invocation process more records at once, which generally increases per-batch processing time rather than reducing ingestion latency. Option E is incorrect because ReportBatchItemFailures controls partial-batch retry behavior for failed records and does not improve throughput or reduce latency for healthy records.

39.text:
Option D is correct because the instance must reliably retrieve its metadata from Amazon S3 every time it starts or relaunches. Using a CloudFormation template that defines the EC2 instance with a UserData script that pulls the metadata from S3 at boot ensures the metadata is fetched automatically on every launch and relaunch. Combined with the instance's status-check recovery behavior, this is a self-contained, declarative solution that satisfies both the metadata-retrieval and automatic-restart requirements without depending on push mechanisms.

Options A and C are incorrect primarily because they rely on an S3 event notification to push metadata to the instance after it comes back up; S3 event notifications signal that an object changed and cannot reliably push files onto a freshly started EC2 instance, so the metadata would not be retrieved on restart. Option C additionally describes EC2 Auto Recovery as stopping and starting rather than the recover action's intent. Option B is incorrect because AWS OpsWorks adds substantial configuration-management overhead for a single instance and is a legacy approach compared with a CloudFormation UserData-driven bootstrap.

40.text:
Option D is correct because the clients are IPv6 and the instances sit in a private subnet behind an Application Load Balancer. Adding an IPv6 CIDR block to the VPC and the ALB subnets and configuring the ALB with the dualstack IP address type lets the load balancer accept both IPv4 and IPv6 client connections on the listener (port 443). The ALB then forwards traffic to the EC2 targets in the target group over the VPC's internal addressing, so the private instances need no public or IPv6 addresses of their own.

Option A is incorrect because assigning IPv6 addresses to the instances themselves does not make a private-subnet, internet-facing service reachable from IPv6 clients; the entry point is the load balancer, which must be dualstack. Option B is incorrect because IPv6 Elastic IP addresses are not how ALB targets receive IPv6 client traffic, and it omits enabling dualstack on the ALB. Option C is incorrect because replacing the ALB with an NLB is unnecessary and an NLB does not use an IPv6 Elastic IP in the manner described; enabling dualstack on the existing ALB is the correct, minimal change.

41.text:
Option C is correct because CloudFormation emits stack lifecycle state changes to Amazon EventBridge, so creating an EventBridge rule that matches the UPDATE_COMPLETE status for the specific stack and targets the Lambda function triggers the tagging logic automatically and only after a successful update. This is event-driven, requires no polling, and scopes the action to the precise stack instance, meeting the requirement cleanly.

Option A is incorrect because there is no native Systems Manager mechanism that detects a CloudFormation UPDATE_COMPLETE event and invokes a runbook in this way; it invents an integration. Option B is incorrect because AWS Config evaluates resource configuration compliance and is not designed to fire on a transient CloudFormation UPDATE_COMPLETE status, and using Config remediation here is a mismatch for reacting to a stack-update event. Option D is incorrect because CloudFormation stack event notifications to SNS cannot be filtered to send only UPDATE_COMPLETE events; the stack publishes all event types, so subscribing the Lambda function to the topic would invoke it on many irrelevant events rather than only after a successful update.

42.text:
Option D is correct because the CloudWatch Logs agent already ships login events to CloudWatch Logs, so a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter can stream matching login events directly to a Lambda function in near real time. That function tags the offending EC2 instance and marks it for decommissioning. A separate EventBridge scheduled rule then invokes a daily Lambda function that terminates all instances carrying the tag, satisfying the requirement to terminate any manually accessed instance within 24 hours in a fully automated way.

Option A is incorrect because inserting AWS Step Functions between the log subscription and the tagging Lambda function adds an orchestration layer with no benefit for this simple tag-then-terminate flow, increasing complexity. Options B and C are incorrect because they depend on humans: option B sends an SNS notification and relies on the operations team to manually terminate instances, which is not automated, and option C routes alarms to an SQS queue processed by a self-managed fleet of worker instances, which is unnecessarily complex and operationally heavy compared with the direct Logs-subscription-to-Lambda pattern.

43.text:
Option B is correct because the dead-letter queue is filling with errorless, already-processed records due to whole-batch retries: when one poison record in a batch fails, the entire batch is retried and eventually sent to the DLQ. Enabling the event source mapping option to split the batch on error (bisect batch on function error) makes Lambda recursively halve the batch to isolate the failing records, so the good records that succeeded are not repeatedly retried or forwarded to the DLQ. This directly reduces the number of successfully processed records reaching the dead-letter queue.

Option A is incorrect because increasing retry attempts would reprocess and re-send the same good records even more times, worsening the problem. Option C is incorrect because increasing concurrent batches per shard improves parallel throughput but does not change how failures within a batch are handled, so errorless records still flow to the DLQ. Option D is incorrect because decreasing the maximum age of a record only changes how long records are eligible for retry before being discarded; it does not isolate the failing record from the good ones in a batch.

44.text:
Options B, E, and D are correct because they assemble a low-overhead deploy-then-test pipeline. Option D updates the build stage to emit an imagedefinitions.json file referencing the new ECR image tag, which is the artifact AWS CodeDeploy and ECS use to know which image to deploy. Option B adds a deploy stage with CodeDeploy as the action provider to roll the new image onto the ECS service. Option E then runs integration tests with the least management overhead by using a serverless Lambda function, invoked as a CodePipeline Lambda action, to perform connectivity checks and API calls against the deployed service endpoint.

Option A is incorrect because using the Amazon ECS action provider performs a standard rolling deployment but does not pair with the CodeDeploy AppSpec-driven flow the scenario builds toward, and it is not the choice that minimizes overhead for the full test integration. Option C is incorrect because an appspec.yml is associated with the CodeDeploy deployment, but on its own it is not one of the three required steps that satisfy building, deploying, and testing here. Option F is incorrect because hosting an integration-test script in S3 and wiring an S3 action requires managing the script artifact and its execution environment, which is more operational overhead than a managed Lambda action.

45.text:
Options A and D are correct because together they create tests and enforce that failures block deployment. Option D creates meaningful infrastructure unit tests using the AWS CDK assertions module, where template.hasResourceProperties verifies that synthesized resources have the expected properties. Option A enforces the gate by running those tests in the CodeBuild build phase before cdk deploy and setting the phase's on-failure behavior to ABORT, so if any test fails the build stops immediately and the deployment does not proceed, exactly satisfying the requirement that deployment continue only when no test fails.

Option B is incorrect because the --rollback true flag controls how a failed deployment is rolled back; it does not prevent deployment from starting when unit tests fail. Option C is incorrect because --require-approval any-change adds a manual approval prompt for security-sensitive changes rather than failing the build on unit-test failures. Option E is incorrect because cdk diff reports differences between deployed and synthesized stacks and is expected to show changes on any update; failing on any diff would block legitimate deployments and does not actually test infrastructure component behavior.

46.text:
Option C is correct because the instances now have no internet access, so the user data script can no longer reach an external artifact source. Publishing the application artifacts to an Amazon S3 bucket and creating a VPC endpoint for S3 lets the instances retrieve artifacts over the private AWS network without any internet route. Assigning an IAM instance profile gives the instances least-privilege permission to read the artifacts from the bucket, so the user data installation succeeds while fully complying with the no-internet requirement.

Option A is incorrect because attaching Elastic IP addresses and disassociating them afterward gives the instances public internet access during install, which violates the new security classification. Option B is incorrect because a NAT gateway exists specifically to provide outbound internet access from a private subnet, so routing through it would again breach the no-internet rule. Option D is incorrect because allowing outbound traffic to an artifact repository still requires internet connectivity, and toggling a security group rule after install is both a manual, fragile process and a violation of the requirement during the install window.

47.text:
Options B, C, and F are correct because they implement attribute-based, least-privilege access through IAM Identity Center with a federated IdP. Option F enables attributes for access control in IAM Identity Center and maps attributes from the external IdP as key-value pairs, so SAML assertions carry the tags used in policies. Option B creates permission sets with an inline policy that uses the aws:PrincipalTag condition key to scope permissions to the team's own resources, applying least privilege. Option C creates a group in the IdP, places users in it, and assigns that group to accounts and permission sets in IAM Identity Center, which is the supported way to grant access when using an external IdP.

Option A is incorrect because directly authoring standalone IAM policies bypasses the permission-set model that IAM Identity Center uses to deliver scoped access. Option D is incorrect because you assign groups to accounts and permission sets, not directly to OUs with raw IAM policies, in IAM Identity Center. Option E is incorrect because, with an external IdP, attributes are mapped from the IdP rather than by applying tags to users within Identity Center, so tagging users directly is not the correct configuration.

48.text:
Option A is correct because emitting a structured JSON object to a CloudWatch log group for each transaction lets CloudWatch Logs Insights query and aggregate the transaction details directly. Logs Insights can render results as a pie chart, and you can add that visualization to an existing CloudWatch dashboard in a few clicks. Because everything stays within CloudWatch, there is no additional data store, ETL, or separate visualization tool to manage, making this the most operationally efficient solution that also satisfies the requirement to integrate with the company's existing CloudWatch dashboards.

Option B is incorrect because routing data to S3, querying with Amazon Athena, and exporting results introduces multiple services and manual export steps, and Athena visualizations do not natively embed into CloudWatch dashboards. Option C is incorrect because AWS X-Ray is built for distributed tracing and latency analysis, not for producing transaction pie charts, and its annotations are not a fit for this reporting use case. Option D is incorrect because adding a Lambda subscription filter to aggregate into DynamoDB builds and maintains extra serverless plumbing that the native Logs Insights query in option A makes unnecessary.

49.text:
Option D is correct because storing user session information in an Amazon ElastiCache cluster externalizes session state from the EC2 instances into a shared, low-latency in-memory data store. Because the immutable blue/green deployments and Auto Scaling events replace instances, any session held locally on an instance is lost when that instance terminates; moving the session to ElastiCache (Redis or Memcached) decouples the session from instance lifecycle, so users remain logged in across scaling events and deployments with minimal operational effort.

Option A is incorrect because there is no "smart sessions" feature on an Application Load Balancer; ALB sticky sessions only pin a user to one instance, which still drops the session when that instance is replaced. Option B is incorrect for the same reason: load balancers do not provide a "session sharing" capability, and stickiness does not persist data across instance termination. Option C is technically possible but not operationally efficient or appropriate, because Amazon S3 is object storage with higher latency than an in-memory cache and is a poor fit for the frequent, low-latency read/write pattern of session management.

50.text:
Option A is correct because the AWSCodeCommitPowerUser managed policy is AWS-managed and cannot be edited directly, so the proper approach is to attach an additional customer-managed policy that adds an explicit Deny. An explicit Deny always overrides any Allow in IAM, so denying the codecommit:GitPush and codecommit:PutFile actions, scoped to the specific repositories with a condition that references the main branch (using the codecommit:References condition key), prevents direct pushes to main while still allowing developers to push to feature branches and open pull requests.

Option B is incorrect because replacing the policy with AWSCodeCommitReadOnly would remove the developers' ability to push to any branch, including feature branches, breaking their workflow; AWS-managed policies also cannot have rules added to them. Option C is incorrect because the AWSCodeCommitPowerUser managed policy is immutable and cannot be modified to include a Deny rule. Option D is incorrect because an Allow rule for feature branches does not restrict the main branch; the existing PowerUser permissions would still permit direct pushes to main since nothing explicitly denies it.

51.text:
Options A and E are correct because they directly address the two distinct workloads. For the critical data that cannot tolerate interruption but needs fast scale-out, Option A adds a warm pool of pre-initialized instances kept in the stopped state, dramatically reducing scale-out latency because bootstrapping is already complete; detailed monitoring provides one-minute metrics for faster scaling reactions. For the noncritical, interruptible, memory-bound workload, Option E creates a second Auto Scaling group using the predefined memory utilization metric for a target tracking policy and uses Spot Instances to reduce cost, registering it as a separate ALB target group.

Option B is incorrect because, although On-Demand suits critical data, a separate option is needed for the noncritical workload, and A already provides the warm pool solution; the pairing of A and E covers both requirements. Option C is incorrect because lifecycle hooks add startup latency rather than reducing scale-out latency, conflicting with the requirement for quick scalability. Option D is incorrect because configuring a custom CloudWatch agent memory metric adds unnecessary operational overhead when Auto Scaling already offers a predefined memory utilization metric type, making E the simpler, more efficient choice.

52.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Control Tower proactive controls are implemented as AWS CloudFormation hooks that evaluate resources before they are provisioned. By enabling a proactive control on the organizational units, any CloudFormation stack that attempts to create an S3 bucket without AWS KMS (SSE-KMS) encryption is blocked at deployment time, directly satisfying the requirement that buckets be encrypted with KMS when created in a CloudFormation stack across the Control Tower environment.

Option C is incorrect because detective controls only identify and report noncompliant resources after they are created; they do not prevent the creation of an unencrypted S3 bucket, so a noncompliant bucket could exist. Option A is incorrect because denying s3:PutObject based on the x-amz-server-side-encryption header governs object upload requests, not the bucket creation/configuration in CloudFormation, and it does not enforce the bucket's default encryption with KMS. Option D is incorrect because an AWS Config rule is detective and reports compliance after the fact rather than proactively preventing the creation of unencrypted buckets in the stack.

53.text:
Option C is correct because the most secure pattern is to remove anonymous/unauthenticated access using an S3 bucket policy and then grant the CodeBuild project's service role IAM permissions to read the object. CodeBuild assumes its service role automatically, so the AWS CLI inside the build can retrieve the script using temporary, automatically rotated credentials with no long-lived secrets to manage, satisfying the security team's prohibition on unauthenticated requests while following least-privilege best practices.

Option A is incorrect because there is no "AllowedBuckets" setting in CodeBuild project configuration; this is a fabricated feature. Option B is incorrect because S3 does not support "HTTPS basic authentication" with a token; S3 uses SigV4 IAM-based request signing, so this approach is invalid. Option D is incorrect because, while it removes anonymous access, embedding a long-lived IAM access key and secret access key in the build is a poor security practice that introduces credential management and rotation risk; using the CodeBuild service role with temporary credentials is more secure.

54.text:
Options A, D, and F are correct because an unencrypted AMI cannot be encrypted in place, so in the source account you must copy the unencrypted AMI to a new encrypted AMI and specify the company's KMS key in the copy operation (Option A). Because the encrypted AMI's snapshots are protected by a KMS key, the target account needs permission to use that key: you modify the source key policy to let the target account create grants, then in the target account create a KMS grant that delegates decrypt/use permissions to the Auto Scaling service-linked role so it can launch instances (Option D). Finally, you share the encrypted AMI with the target account (Option F).

Option B is incorrect because the default EBS encryption key is an AWS-managed key that cannot be shared across accounts, so the target account could not decrypt the snapshots; a customer managed KMS key is required. Option C is incorrect because it omits the necessary step of modifying the source key policy to allow the target account to create grants, so the grant alone would fail. Option E is incorrect because sharing the unencrypted AMI violates the requirement that all shared AMIs be encrypted.

55.text:
Option A is correct because the company already has an external SAML 2.0 identity provider backed by Active Directory, so the DevOps engineer should configure that external IdP as the identity source in IAM Identity Center. Automatic provisioning of users and groups must use the SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) protocol, which is the standard IAM Identity Center supports for synchronizing users and AD groups from an external IdP. SCIM makes the AD groups available in Identity Center for permission-set assignment, while SAML handles the sign-in/authentication flow with corporate credentials.

Option D is incorrect because SAML is used for authentication assertions, not for provisioning users and groups; IAM Identity Center uses SCIM, not SAML, to synchronize identities. Option B is incorrect because the company wants to use its existing external SAML IdP rather than AWS Directory Service as the identity source, and provisioning is not done via SAML. Option C is incorrect because AD Connector proxies directory requests to an on-premises AD over a trust connection and is not the mechanism for an external SAML IdP, and provisioning from an AD Connector source does not use SCIM in this scenario.

56.text:
Option A is correct because packaging the legacy application's dependencies into a custom Docker image stored in Amazon ECR, then using an AWS CodeBuild project that runs that image to build the artifact and upload it to the existing S3 bucket, is fully managed and serverless. CodeBuild provisions and tears down build environments on demand, so there is no infrastructure to patch or scale, and using a custom image guarantees the legacy dependencies are present, making this the most operationally efficient build automation pipeline.

Option B is incorrect because a manually managed EC2 instance requires ongoing patching, scaling, and lifecycle maintenance, and an always-on instance for occasional builds is wasteful and operationally heavy. Option C is incorrect because EC2 Image Builder plus a launched EC2 instance still leaves you managing and running instances to perform builds, which is more overhead than the serverless CodeBuild approach. Option D is incorrect because standing up an EKS cluster with Fargate just to build an artifact is significant unnecessary complexity and operational burden for a simple build-and-upload task, far exceeding what the requirement needs.

57.text:
Option A is correct because the CodeBuild service role in the centralized DevOps account must assume a deployment role in the application account to reach the cross-account EKS cluster. You configure the application account's deployment role with a trust relationship that allows sts:AssumeRole from the DevOps account, grant that role the required EKS access, and then map the role in the EKS cluster's aws-auth ConfigMap to Kubernetes RBAC permissions. EKS authorization is governed by the aws-auth ConfigMap, so without mapping the IAM principal there, the connection returns Unauthorized even with valid IAM permissions.

Option D is incorrect because the deployment role should trust the centralized DevOps account where CodeBuild runs, not the Control Tower management account; trusting the management account would not let the CodeBuild role assume it. Options B and C are incorrect because they place the trust and access configuration on the DevOps account's role rather than the application account's role, and they never update the EKS aws-auth ConfigMap, so the cross-account principal would still be unauthorized in the cluster. Option C additionally misuses sts:AssumeRoleWithSAML, which is for SAML federation, not service-to-service role assumption.

58.text:
Option C is correct because it meets every requirement with managed services. Using EC2 Image Builder to rebuild the custom AMI with the latest Systems Manager Agent, and attaching the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore instance profile, lets the instances register with Systems Manager over the existing VPC endpoints without internet access. Systems Manager Session Manager provides centralized, automated, auditable login without bastion hosts or open inbound ports, and session details are logged to Amazon S3. An S3 event notification on new session-log uploads publishes to an SNS topic, notifying the security team whenever instances are accessed.

Option A is incorrect because it tries to deploy VPC routes for external internet access, which violates the requirement that the sensitive instances have no internet access, and EC2 Instance Connect notifications are not as cleanly centralized. Option B is incorrect because deploying a NAT gateway and a bastion host adds internet exposure and operational overhead, contradicting the no-internet requirement. Option D is incorrect because AWS Config does not attach SCPs (that is AWS Organizations), and the described configuration mechanism is invalid, so the solution would not function as stated.

59.text:
Option D is correct because it provides a fully automated, event-driven promotion with a clean reconnection mechanism. Storing the Aurora endpoint in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store gives a single source of truth for the connection string. An Amazon EventBridge rule detects the RDS failure event and invokes an AWS Lambda function that promotes the cross-Region read replica to primary and updates the endpoint value in Parameter Store. Because the application is coded to reload the endpoint from Parameter Store on connection failure, it automatically picks up the new primary without redeployment.

Option A is incorrect because a latency-based Route 53 CNAME pointing at both endpoints does not control which database is writable, and promoting a replica does not change DNS automatically in a safe way; this risks routing writes to a read replica. Option B is incorrect because Aurora custom endpoints route within a single cluster and cannot point at a cross-Region replica, and CloudTrail does not "run" Lambda functions directly. Option C is incorrect because mutating the CloudFormation template at failover time to promote a replica is slow, fragile, and operationally complex compared to direct API promotion.

60.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose natively supports inline data transformation through an AWS Lambda transformer, allowing the flow logs to be modified and enriched with additional data before delivery. By changing the AWS Network Firewall logging destination from S3 directly to a Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream that targets the existing S3 bucket, the records are transformed in the streaming path and then written to the same bucket, meeting the requirement with a managed, buffered delivery pipeline that integrates with Athena downstream.

Option A is incorrect because triggering a Lambda function on object-create events in the same bucket it writes back to creates a recursive invocation loop that AWS specifically warns against; acknowledging the recursion does not make it a sound design. Option B is incorrect because routing through a custom EventBridge bus and Step Functions to write to a new bucket adds significant complexity and does not deliver back into the existing bucket as required. Option C is incorrect because EventBridge input transformation only reshapes the event payload passed to a target; it cannot transform and enrich the actual log file contents and write them to S3.

61.text:
Options A, E, and F are correct because together they provide the required replication SLA and a controlled failover mechanism. S3 Replication Time Control (S3 RTC) on each replication rule guarantees that objects are replicated within 15 minutes and provides replication metrics, satisfying the time requirement (Option A). For failover, Amazon Route 53 Application Recovery Controller routing controls let you model the buckets in an active-passive configuration (Option E), and you flip traffic deliberately by calling the UpdateRoutingControlStates API to change the routing control state during a failover (Option F).

Option B is incorrect because an S3 Multi-Region Access Point in active-passive failover handles request routing across Regions but is paired with the SubmitMultiRegionAccessPointRoutes API, and the question's chosen failover mechanism is the Route 53 Recovery Controller path. Option C is incorrect for the same reason: SubmitMultiRegionAccessPointRoutes belongs to the Multi-Region Access Point approach, not the Recovery Controller routing control approach selected here. Option D is incorrect because S3 Transfer Acceleration only speeds up uploads over long distances via edge locations; it has no role in cross-Region replication timing or failover.

62.text:
Option D is correct because it combines the right managed services to cover security, summarized visibility, and rapid investigation with minimal log management. Amazon GuardDuty EKS Audit Log Monitoring continuously analyzes EKS audit logs for malicious activity without you managing the logs, AWS CloudTrail captures API calls and login attempts, VPC Flow Logs enable network traffic analysis, and CloudWatch Container Insights provides summarized resource behavior and metrics across the EKS workload. GuardDuty findings let the security team quickly investigate suspicious behavior tied to the cluster.

Option A is incorrect because building an S3 plus Athena plus QuickSight pipeline for EKS audit and CloudTrail logs adds substantial log-management overhead, which the requirement explicitly seeks to minimize. Option B is incorrect because, although GuardDuty and Detective aid investigation, this option omits VPC Flow Logs and CloudTrail-based network/API coverage needed for full network traffic analysis and the summarized cross-environment view. Option C is incorrect because Container Insights alone is monitoring, not threat detection; without GuardDuty it cannot detect unauthorized logins or malicious behavior, and the Athena/QuickSight pipeline again increases log-management overhead.

63.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP is a fully managed shared file system that natively supports both SMB (for Windows) and NFS (for Linux) protocols against the same data, satisfying the mixed-protocol shared-storage requirement. For the pilot light DR environment, NetApp SnapMirror replication efficiently and continuously replicates the file system from the primary Region to the FSx for ONTAP instance in the DR Region, so the automated EC2 instances launched there can mount the replicated storage.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage and does not present SMB or NFS file-share semantics that the migrated applications require. Option B is incorrect because Amazon EBS volumes are single-instance block storage, not shared SMB/NFS file systems, and snapshot replication does not provide live shared file access. Option C is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway Volume Gateway presents iSCSI block volumes, not SMB/NFS file shares, and Volume Gateway does not offer the dual-protocol shared file access the applications need.

64.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Step Functions is purpose-built to orchestrate multistep sequential workflows, modeling each processing step as a state with built-in error handling, retries, and checkpointing. By decoupling each compute step into a Step Functions task backed by AWS Lambda (each step completes well within the 15-minute Lambda limit since steps run in 5 minutes or less), the workflow can resume from a failed step rather than reprocessing the record from the beginning, and the serverless model removes the operational burden of managing instances.

Option A is incorrect because chaining S3 events, SNS, and an EC2 polling instance is a brittle, manually managed pattern with no native per-step retry/resume orchestration and added instance management overhead. Option B is incorrect because having a Fargate container "invoke itself" to pass state is not a real orchestration mechanism and provides no reliable per-step failure recovery. Option C is incorrect because Kinesis Data Streams plus Lambda decouples ingestion and throughput but does not natively track sequential step state, so resuming only the failed step would require custom-built state management, increasing complexity.

65.text:
Option A is correct because CloudFormation cross-stack references using stack Exports and Fn::ImportValue let the two teams keep entirely separate templates, review processes, and lifecycle management. The database team exports resource references (such as ARNs or IDs) from their stack, and the web application team imports those values into their own stack. Each team retains independent change-set reviews at the resource level, and the software development team can deploy their template through their own CI/CD pipeline without coupling to the database team's deployment process.

Option B is incorrect because a nested stack would place the database resources under the parent (web application) stack's lifecycle, breaking the requirement that the database team keep its own independent review and lifecycle management. Option C is incorrect because StackSets are designed to deploy the same template across multiple accounts or Regions, not to share resource references between two distinct, independently managed stacks. Option D is incorrect because manually passing resource names and IDs as input parameters is brittle and not automatically maintained; it lacks the formal export/import linkage and does not cleanly preserve independent, resource-level change-set reviews across the two pipelines.

66.text:
