Option B is incorrect because GetLoginProfile is a read call that does not signal new user creation. Option D is incorrect because it only deletes the login profile and leaves access keys active, failing to fully disable the user's credentials. Option F is incorrect because Amazon SQS does not support email subscriptions; SQS is a message queue, not a notification service, so it cannot deliver alerts to the security team's inbox the way SNS does.

134.text:
Option B is correct because both CodePipeline and CodeDeploy emit state-change events to Amazon EventBridge, including deployment and pipeline failures. An EventBridge rule can match those failure events and invoke a Lambda function to evaluate the specific deployment issue, then publish to an SNS topic that notifies stakeholders. This near-real-time, event-driven pattern decreases resolution time exactly as required.

Option A is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs collects log data and AWS Config evaluates resource configuration compliance; neither natively detects CodeDeploy deployment failures to drive timely notifications. Option C is incorrect because CloudTrail records API activity primarily for auditing and is delayed and noisy compared with EventBridge state-change events for deployment monitoring. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs vulnerability and security assessments of workloads and has no role in evaluating CodePipeline or CodeDeploy deployment failures, so an Inspector assessment target cannot generate the deployment issue notifications.

135.text:
Option B is correct because AWS-scheduled EC2 maintenance is published as an AWS Health event, and Amazon EventBridge can match those Health maintenance events and route them to an SNS topic. Subscribing a Lambda function to the topic lets the function enrich the message by looking up the affected instances' Name and Owner tags and then post formatted notifications to both the Slack channel and the shared inbox, meeting all requirements with native event integration.

Option A is incorrect because Trusted Advisor and AWS Config evaluate configuration and best practices, not scheduled EC2 maintenance events. Option C is incorrect because CloudWatch metrics and alarms monitor numeric performance data and do not surface scheduled maintenance notifications, so the alarm would have nothing to trigger on. Option D is incorrect because CloudTrail records API calls and there is no native "AWS Support integration with CloudTrail" mechanism that converts EC2 maintenance notifications into events, making this approach unworkable.

136.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Config continuously evaluates resource configurations against rules, so Config rules can check every existing and newly created S3 bucket for encryption, logging, versioning, and public access. By attaching automatic remediation that runs AWS Systems Manager Automation documents, any noncompliant bucket is corrected without manual intervention. This continuously enforces the security baseline across daily new buckets and all existing ones.

Option A is incorrect because CloudTrail records API activity but does not evaluate bucket configuration state, and building all remediation logic in Lambda is more operational overhead than using Config's managed rules and SSM remediation. Option C is incorrect because Trusted Advisor offers periodic best-practice checks rather than continuous configuration evaluation with auto-remediation, and EventBridge alone does not remediate. Option D is incorrect because Systems Manager manages and automates instances and operations but does not continuously detect noncompliant S3 bucket configurations the way AWS Config does, so it cannot reliably enforce the requirements.

137.text:
Option B is correct because a CloudFormation custom resource backed by Lambda relies on the Lambda function sending a response to the pre-signed S3 URL that CloudFormation provides in the event payload. CloudFormation waits on that callback to learn whether the resource succeeded or failed; if the function never sends SUCCESS or FAILED to the pre-signed URL, the stack remains stuck in CREATE_IN_PROGRESS until it times out, which is exactly the symptom described even though AD Connector was created.

Option A is incorrect because the function exiting successfully is not sufficient; CloudFormation has no way to know the outcome without the explicit response to the pre-signed URL. Option C is incorrect because custom resources do not require cloudformation:UpdateStack permissions to signal completion. Option D is incorrect because while ds:ConnectDirectory permissions are needed to create AD Connector, the resource was already created successfully, so missing directory permissions is not the cause of the stuck CREATE_IN_PROGRESS state.

138.text:
Options B and D are correct. IAM Roles Anywhere lets the on-premises CI/CD pipeline obtain temporary AWS credentials using its existing X.509 certificates through a trust anchor and an IAM role scoped to CodeArtifact actions, so the pipeline can publish packages securely without long-lived access keys, minimizing operational overhead. For public dependencies, creating a CodeArtifact repository per public repository with an external connection and configuring those as upstream repositories lets CodeArtifact fetch and cache public packages on demand, making them available to application pipelines.

Option A is incorrect because building VM images and using AWS Import/Export to create AMIs solely to publish packages is heavyweight and operationally complex. Option C is incorrect because routing packages through an S3 bucket with presigned URLs and a Lambda relay adds unnecessary moving parts compared with publishing directly via Roles Anywhere. Option E is incorrect because external connections to public repositories are configured as upstream sources, not downstream, so this describes the relationship backwards and would not provide the public packages.

139.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Config continuously discovers and evaluates resources, so a Config rule that checks whether VPC flow logs are enabled will catch every existing VPC and any new VPC regardless of which IAM user or which CloudFormation stack created it. Configuring automatic remediation to turn on flow logs ensures the requirement is enforced account-wide without depending on how a VPC was provisioned.

Option A is incorrect because adding the AWS::EC2::FlowLog resource to the CloudFormation stack only covers VPCs created by that stack, not VPCs that any IAM user creates by other means. Option B is incorrect because an SCP that prevents modifying flow logs does not create flow logs on VPCs that never had them, and it does not enforce enablement on new VPCs. Option D is incorrect because an IAM deny policy on flow log API calls would block legitimate management and still would not enable flow logs on VPCs that lack them.

140.text:
Option D is correct because EC2 resource creation API calls are recorded by CloudTrail and delivered to EventBridge as events with Amazon EC2 as the source. An EventBridge rule matching those CloudTrail-delivered EC2 events can target the Lambda function immediately upon resource creation. The Lambda function then reads the creating user's identity from the event and applies the user ID and cost center tags well within the one-hour window, in a fully event-driven manner.

Option A is incorrect because s3:ObjectTagging:Put events relate to tagging S3 objects, not creating EC2 resources, and bucket versioning is irrelevant. Option B is incorrect for the same reason; server access logging and S3 object tagging events do not detect EC2 resource creation. Option C is incorrect because an hourly scheduled Lambda that parses CloudTrail logs from S3 is slower, more complex, and risks missing the one-hour requirement, whereas the EventBridge rule reacts to creation events in near real time.

141.text:
Option A is correct because StackSets fail when a stack attempts to enable GuardDuty in an account where a detector already exists, since GuardDuty allows only one detector per account per Region. A CloudFormation custom resource backed by a Lambda function can first check whether GuardDuty is already enabled and conditionally enable it only when no detector exists, so the deployment succeeds across the mixed estate without failing on accounts that already have GuardDuty.

Option B is incorrect because the CloudFormation Conditions section evaluates only static template inputs and parameters, not the live runtime state of GuardDuty in each target account, so it cannot detect existing detectors. Option C is incorrect because Fn::GetAtt returns attributes of resources defined within the same template and cannot query whether GuardDuty is already enabled in an account. Option D is incorrect because manually discovering account IDs is not scalable, and Fn::ImportValue imports exported stack output values, not a dynamic list of accounts to skip.

142.text:
Option B is correct because CloudFront origin groups provide built-in origin failover with effectively zero RTO for HTTP GET requests. By adding the secondary Region's ALB as a second origin, creating an origin group with the original ALB as primary, and configuring failover on HTTP 5xx status codes, CloudFront automatically retries the request against the secondary origin when the primary returns a server error. Updating the default behavior to use the origin group makes this failover transparent to clients without any DNS propagation delay.

Option A is incorrect because Route 53 failover between two CloudFront distributions depends on DNS resolution and health-check timing, which cannot guarantee a zero-second RTO. Option C is incorrect for the same reason; even with TTL 0, DNS-based failover incurs detection and resolution delays. Option D is incorrect because a CloudFront function returning a 307 redirect pushes failover handling onto the client and is not a reliable, automatic origin failover mechanism, so it does not meet the zero-RTO requirement.

143.text:
Option B is correct because Aurora cross-Region automated backup replication continuously copies backups to the DR Region, easily meeting the 8-hour RPO, and ECR cross-Region replication keeps the already-built Docker images present in the DR Region. Because the prebuilt image is available locally, recovery does not require rebuilding from the Dockerfile, which can exceed two hours, so launching the CloudFormation stack from the replicated snapshot and image meets the 2-hour RTO cost-effectively without running idle standby compute.

Option A is incorrect because building the Docker image during a DR event can take more than two hours, violating the RTO. Option C is incorrect because hand-built Lambda-and-EventBridge logic to snapshot and copy images adds operational overhead and complexity compared with native cross-Region replication, and it still risks the RTO. Option D is incorrect because deploying a second full standby stack with an Aurora global database is more expensive than warm backups and exceeds what the 8-hour RPO and 2-hour RTO require.

144.text:
Option B is correct because S3 server access logging records detailed request-level information, including the requester, object key, and request type, which captures exactly the access pattern the company needs. Using Amazon Athena to define an external table over the log files in S3 lets the company run SQL queries directly against the logs without loading them into a database or managing any infrastructure, providing the analysis with the least effort.

Option A is incorrect because importing access logs into an Aurora database requires provisioning, loading, and managing a relational database, which is far more effort than querying logs in place with Athena. Option C is incorrect because invoking a Lambda function for every object access and writing to Aurora is operationally heavy and adds per-request latency and cost. Option D is incorrect because building a CloudWatch Logs pipeline feeding a Kinesis Data Analytics SQL application for sliding-window analysis is complex streaming infrastructure that exceeds what this straightforward access-pattern reporting requires.

145.text:
Option A is correct because CloudFormation StackSets deployed from a delegated administrator account is the native, operationally efficient mechanism to push the same Lambda function to all 500 member accounts at once, including future accounts through automatic deployment. Centralizing the schedule in an EventBridge event bus in the delegated administrator account lets one source invoke the Lambda function in each member account on the required 30-minute cadence without per-account scripting or maintenance.

Option B is incorrect because writing a custom script in the management account to assume a role and deploy CloudFormation to every account is brittle, manual, and far higher overhead than StackSets; attaching FullAccess policies is also poor least-privilege practice. Option C is plausible but less efficient at scale because placing an independent EventBridge scheduled rule in every member account creates 500 schedules to manage and drift, whereas A centralizes invocation. Option D is incorrect because CodeDeploy does not deploy Lambda functions across an organization this way, and AmazonS3FullAccess and CodeDeployDeployerAccess grant irrelevant, overly broad permissions.

146.text:
Option C is correct because an Amazon SQS dead-letter queue (DLQ) is purpose-built to capture messages that cannot be processed successfully. By adding a redrive policy to the source queue that sets maxReceiveCount (Maximum Receives) and references the DLQ ARN, any message the transformation application repeatedly fails to process is automatically moved to the DLQ. This retains the failed messages durably and makes them available for scientists to review and reprocess later, with no custom code.

Option A is incorrect because it introduces a complex, fully custom Lambda polling-and-replay pipeline to S3, which is far more operational overhead than the built-in DLQ feature and reinvents what SQS provides natively. Option B is incorrect because converting to a FIFO queue does not address failed-message retention, and using SentTimestamp age to move messages misclassifies merely slow messages as failures rather than capturing genuine processing failures. Option D is incorrect because SQS virtual queues are a client-side library construct for multiplexing, not a native retention mechanism for failed messages, and it requires invasive application and API Gateway changes.

147.text:
Options B, D, and F are correct because they collectively capture both application logs and ALB access logs and stream them to S3 for near-real-time analysis. Configuring the ECS task definition to use the awslogs logging driver (B) sends container application logs to CloudWatch Logs. Activating access logging on the ALB and pointing it to the S3 bucket (D) delivers the access logs natively. Creating a Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream to the S3 bucket and attaching a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter to it (F) continuously streams the application logs from CloudWatch Logs into S3 in near real time.

Option A is incorrect because there is no downloadable "CloudWatch Logs container instance" task; the awslogs driver is the supported mechanism. Option C is incorrect because the create-export-task command runs batch exports that can take time and cannot run more frequently than every few minutes, so it is neither near-real-time nor efficient. Option E is incorrect because access logging is enabled on the load balancer, not on target groups; target groups do not produce access logs.

148.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon EKS can publish control plane and component logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs, and a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter delivers matching log events in near real time directly to an AWS Lambda function. By creating one subscription filter per component, each filter forwards only the relevant events to the Lambda function, which can then evaluate them and publish to the correct SNS topic for targeted email notifications. This is the native, event-driven logging-to-Lambda pattern.

Option B is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs Insights is an interactive, on-demand query tool; it does not stream events automatically to EventBridge to trigger Lambda, so it cannot drive real-time per-event notifications. Options C and D are incorrect because EKS components log to CloudWatch Logs, not to S3, so "S3 logging for the EKS components" is not a supported source. Additionally, in C, subscription filters apply to CloudWatch Logs rather than S3, and in D, S3 PUT event notifications would only react to object uploads rather than evaluate individual log events.

149.text:
Option C is correct because in a CodeDeploy blue/green deployment to Amazon ECS, the AppSpec file's hooks section supports lifecycle event hooks that invoke Lambda functions. The AfterAllowTestTraffic hook runs after the green (replacement) task set is registered with a test listener but before production traffic is shifted, which is exactly when validation should occur. Running the test scripts there and exiting the Lambda function with an error signals failure to CodeDeploy, which automatically initiates a rollback before any customer traffic is affected.

Option D is incorrect because AfterAllowTraffic runs after production traffic has already been shifted to the green version, so failures would impact users; using stop-deployment at that point is also not the clean validation-gate mechanism. Options A and B are incorrect because adding a CodeBuild or Lambda test stage between source and deploy runs the tests before deployment, so it cannot test the green version that does not yet exist; stop-deployment does not perform the test-traffic validation and rollback that the ECS blue/green lifecycle hooks provide.

150.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Elastic Beanstalk natively supports the Go platform and provides built-in deployment management, including blue/green deployments (by swapping environment CNAMEs) and traffic-splitting deployments for A/B-style canary testing, with minimal operational effort. Storing a zipped application version in Amazon S3 and pointing Elastic Beanstalk at it to deploy new versions matches the team's requested workflow while letting Elastic Beanstalk handle capacity, load balancing, and deployment orchestration.

Option A is incorrect because a hand-built AMI plus Auto Scaling launch configuration with instance refresh provides rolling replacement but no native blue/green swap or A/B traffic-splitting capability, and it carries high operational overhead. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Lightsail is a simplified VPS offering aimed at simple workloads and does not provide managed blue/green or A/B testing deployment options. Option C is incorrect because CodeArtifact is an artifact repository for packages, not application source storage for deployments; while CodeDeploy supports blue/green, this combination is more complex and does not directly deliver the A/B testing capability Elastic Beanstalk offers out of the box.

151.text:
Options B and D are correct because they implement the MD5 integrity verification the script needs. Passing the locally computed MD5 in the Content-MD5 request header makes Amazon S3 reject the PutObject call if the received data does not match, so checking the operation's return status reveals any corruption error (B). For objects uploaded with a single PutObject (not multipart) and without SSE-KMS, S3 returns the object's MD5 digest as the ETag in the response; comparing that returned ETag against the locally computed MD5 confirms the data was stored intact before the on-premises copy is deleted (D).

Option A is incorrect because VersionId is an opaque version identifier that has no relationship to the content's MD5 checksum, so comparing them is meaningless. Option C is incorrect because the tagging parameter stores key-value tags for the object and is not a data-integrity verification mechanism. Option E is incorrect because storing the checksum as user metadata and reading it back with HeadObject only confirms what you uploaded was preserved as metadata; it does not verify that the object payload itself was transmitted without corruption.

152.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Config supports custom rules backed by a Lambda function that can evaluate KMS key resources and their rotation state. By developing a custom Config rule that flags keys whose last rotation is more than 90 days old as noncompliant, the rule continuously evaluates the keys, and AWS Config can publish compliance change notifications to an Amazon SNS topic to alert the security team. This provides the ongoing, programmable compliance check that manual-rotation tracking requires.

Option A is incorrect because AWS KMS does not natively publish age-based or rotation-overdue events to SNS; it has no built-in "keys older than 90 days" notification. Option B is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor does not provide a check for manual KMS key rotation age, so the Lambda function would have no relevant data to act on. Option D is incorrect because AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from security services against standards but does not itself emit a 90-day manual key-rotation alert to SNS for this custom requirement.

153.text:
Options A, B, and E are correct because Amazon Inspector relies on the AWS Systems Manager Agent (SSM Agent) to gather software inventory from EC2 instances for vulnerability scanning. The SSM Agent must be installed and running on each instance (A), the instance must be able to reach the Systems Manager service endpoints, which requires outbound HTTPS (port 443) connectivity through its security group (B), and the instance must have an IAM instance profile granting the permissions needed to communicate with Systems Manager (E). Without all three, Systems Manager cannot manage the instance, so Inspector lists it as "not scanning."

Option C is incorrect because inspector:StartAssessmentRun relates to the classic Inspector assessment workflow and the engineer's own permissions, not why managed instances fail to be scanned. Option D is incorrect because EC2 Instance Connect provides SSH access and is unrelated to Inspector scanning. Option F is incorrect because managed-instance activations are for registering on-premises or non-EC2 hybrid machines; EC2 instances use an instance profile rather than an activation code and ID.

154.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager lets you create a custom patch baseline that can include a custom (alternate) repository in addition to the default sources, which is exactly what the requirement to use both a default and a custom repository calls for. Running the AWS-RunPatchBaseline document with Run Command (or on a Maintenance Window schedule) scans and installs both operating system and application patches according to that baseline, providing the continuous, automated compliance the privacy requirements demand.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect is a network connectivity service and has nothing to do with patch repository integration or automated patch deployment. Option C is incorrect because manually using yum-config-manager on each instance is a non-scalable, non-automated approach that provides no centralized compliance reporting across the fleet. Option D is incorrect because AWS-AmazonLinuxDefaultPatchBaseline is a predefined default baseline, and the correct way to verify and install patches against a custom baseline is the AWS-RunPatchBaseline document, not a default-baseline-specific document.

155.text:
Option D is correct because the deployment used CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime in-place against an Auto Scaling group, and Auto Scaling launches instances from the existing launch template/configuration, which references the previous application revision. If Auto Scaling scaled out and launched two new instances after the deployment had already passed those instances (or while the deployment was still in progress), those instances would come up running the old revision rather than the new one. The deployment still reports success because it only acts on the instances present when it ran, explaining why two of five instances have the prior revision.

Option A is incorrect because instances that fail to fetch the new revision would fail the deployment, not allow it to finish successfully. Option B is incorrect because a failed AfterInstall hook would mark those instances as failed and, with OneAtATime, surface a deployment failure rather than a silent success. Option C is incorrect because instances without the CodeDeploy agent would not be reported as successfully deployed; the agent is required for the deployment lifecycle to complete on each instance.

156.text:
Option D is correct because Account Factory for Terraform (AFT) includes a built-in feature flag, aft_feature_enterprise_support, that automatically enrolls newly provisioned accounts in the AWS Enterprise Support plan. Setting this flag to True in the AFT deployment input configuration and redeploying AFT causes the account-provisioning pipeline to enable Enterprise Support for each new account, directly resolving the issue of new accounts defaulting to Basic Support.

Option A is incorrect because the account-part-of-organizations Config rule only checks organization membership and has no capability to change an account's support plan. Option B is incorrect because there is no support:ResolveCase-based automation that adds an account to Enterprise Support, and creating tickets is a manual, unreliable workaround rather than the native AFT capability. Option C is incorrect because there is no AWSEnterpriseSupport parameter in control_tower_parameters, and setting it to the management account number is not a valid configuration; AFT exposes this behavior through its dedicated feature flag instead.

157.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CodeBuild can run the unit tests as a test stage within CodePipeline, and CodeBuild has a native test reporting feature. By defining the test commands in the buildspec.yml build phase to emit results in the JUnitXML report format and configuring a reports section that uploads results to a CodeBuild report group, the company gets structured, viewable test reports (pass/fail trends and details) in the CodeBuild console. This integrates testing into the pipeline and produces the required reports with managed tooling.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CodeGuru Reviewer performs automated code reviews for quality and security recommendations; it does not run unit tests or generate unit-test reports. Option C is incorrect because CodeArtifact is a package repository, not a test report destination, and CodeBuild test reports do not get "sent" there; CUCUMBERJSON is also not the natural choice for Python unit tests here. Option D is incorrect because dumping raw HTML to an S3 bucket bypasses CodeBuild's managed report groups, providing no aggregated, queryable test reporting in the console.

158.text:
Option A is correct because the build failure is an authentication problem with Amazon ECR, not an IAM permissions problem. Even with a correctly scoped IAM service role, the Docker client must obtain a valid registry authentication token before it can push to a private ECR repository. The fix is to update buildspec.yml to run aws ecr get-login-password to retrieve a token and pipe it into docker login (against the ECR registry endpoint) so the subsequent docker push is authenticated. This is the standard ECR login flow in CodeBuild.

Option B is incorrect because a SECRETS_MANAGER environment variable containing the IAM role ARN is not how Docker authenticates to ECR; the role ARN is not a credential, and docker login does not consume it. Option C is incorrect because making the repository public is an inappropriate, insecure change that does not address the push authentication for a private repo and was not requested. Option D is incorrect because the CodeBuild job already runs as the IAM service role, so re-assuming it adds nothing, and the missing step is the docker login authentication, not a repository policy.

159.text:
Options B, D, and E are correct because together they build a CI/CD pipeline that tracks deployment progression and supports automatic rollback. Setting up AWS CodePipeline with the code repository as its source (D) provides the orchestration and stage-by-stage visibility into the deployment's progress. Using AWS CodeBuild to build the artifact into S3 and AWS CodeDeploy to deploy it to the EC2 instances (E) replaces the manual build-and-unzip steps and enables CodeDeploy's automatic rollback on failure. Putting the clear-cache command in a custom script invoked by the BeforeInstall lifecycle hook in the AppSpec file (B) reliably clears the local cache as part of each managed deployment.

Option A is incorrect because using a Lambda function triggered on pulls to build artifacts is ad hoc, bypasses the pipeline's tracking and rollback, and EventBridge does not natively trigger on repository pull events this way. Option C is incorrect because baking the cache-clear into EC2 user data only runs at instance launch, not on each deployment, and "deploy again" is manual with no rollback. Option F is incorrect because using Systems Manager to fetch and deploy the artifact bypasses CodeDeploy's lifecycle hooks and automatic rollback.

160.text:
Options B, C, and E are correct because they form the lowest-effort serverless analytics stack over CSV data in S3. Using an AWS Glue crawler to populate the AWS Glue Data Catalog (E) automatically infers and stores the table schema and metadata from the .csv files, satisfying the persistent, automated metadata store requirement. Amazon Athena (C) then queries the data in place in S3 using standard SQL, reading the table definitions directly from the Glue Data Catalog with no servers to manage. Amazon QuickSight (B) connects to Athena to visualize the query results and generate the graphs the developers need.

Option A is incorrect because AWS X-Ray is a distributed-tracing service for analyzing application requests, not a data-visualization tool. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Redshift would require provisioning and loading data into a cluster, adding significant effort compared to querying S3 directly with Athena. Option F is incorrect because Amazon DynamoDB is a key-value/document database and is not the automated schema/metadata catalog that Glue provides for CSV files in S3.

161.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CloudFormation StackSets supports automatic deployment to accounts as they are created in or moved into target organizational units. By creating a stack set in the management account that enables AWS Config and configuring it with automatic (service-managed) deployment, every future account created in the organization automatically receives the Config setup without any manual intervention, scaling cleanly from 10 to 500 accounts across multiple OUs.

Option A is incorrect because enabling trusted access for AWS Config at the organization level does not, by itself, turn on and configure the AWS Config recorder and delivery channel in each new member account. Option C is incorrect because a service control policy (SCP) only sets permission guardrails; it can allow but never automatically perform the API calls to enable AWS Config. Option D is incorrect because an EventBridge rule on CreateAccount invoking a Systems Manager Automation runbook is a custom, higher-maintenance pattern that you must build and operate yourself, whereas StackSets auto-deployment is the native, lower-overhead solution for this requirement.

162.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling provides built-in notifications that can publish to an Amazon SNS topic for specific fleet events, including the EC2_INSTANCE_LAUNCH_ERROR event that fires when an instance fails to launch. Subscribing the support team's email to that SNS topic delivers an immediate email whenever a failed instance launch occurs, directly addressing the need to be alerted quickly instead of discovering the problem hours later.

Option A is incorrect because Auto Scaling health checks act on running instances that become impaired and trigger replacement; they are not designed to notify on launch failures, and wiring a Lambda function into them is unnecessary complexity. Option C is incorrect because AttachInstances is an API for manually attaching existing instances to a group and is unrelated to automatic launch failures, so an alarm on it would not catch the described problem. Option D is incorrect because EC2 status check alarms monitor the health of instances that have already started, not failures that prevent instances from launching in the first place.

163.text:
Option B is correct because it consolidates both data sets in one queryable place. The Amazon CloudWatch agent ships application logs from the EC2 instances to Amazon CloudWatch Logs, and AWS CloudTrail can be configured to deliver account API activity directly to a CloudWatch Logs log group. With both log sources in CloudWatch Logs, CloudWatch Logs Insights provides a single purpose-built query engine to search and analyze application logs and API activity together, meeting the requirement efficiently.

Option A is incorrect because delivering CloudTrail logs only to Amazon S3 leaves them outside CloudWatch Logs, so you cannot query both sets together in CloudWatch as stated. Option C is incorrect because routing logs through Amazon Kinesis into Amazon Redshift adds substantial ingestion and data-warehouse overhead for what is a straightforward log-query need. Option D is incorrect because while Amazon Athena can query logs in S3, the CloudWatch agent does not send logs directly to S3, making this combination more complex and not the intended native path for querying application and API logs.

164.text:
Option C is correct because the five Lambda functions are independent, so the deployment slowness comes from CodePipeline running their build/test/deploy actions sequentially. CodePipeline runs actions within the same stage in parallel when they are assigned the same runOrder value. Configuring the actions for each Lambda function to share a runOrder lets them execute concurrently, dramatically reducing total pipeline duration without changing the build logic.

Option A is incorrect because the bottleneck is sequential execution, not network throughput, so a higher-bandwidth compute type would not meaningfully speed up the overall pipeline. Option B is incorrect because a single CodeBuild build for one function does not benefit from a symmetric-multiprocessing custom environment here; the functions are separate builds that simply need to run in parallel, which is a pipeline-orchestration change, not a build-environment one. Option D is incorrect because running builds in a VPC on dedicated instances adds cost and complexity (and VPC networking can even slow startup) without addressing the sequential ordering that causes the delay.

165.text:
Option D is correct because deploying an Amazon API Gateway stage emits a CreateDeployment event from the aws.apigateway source, which Amazon EventBridge can react to. Configuring the rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function that downloads the freshly generated JavaScript SDK from API Gateway, uploads it to the S3 bucket, and then refreshes the cached path makes the new SDK available automatically on every deployment, eliminating the manual console export step.

Option C is a close distractor but is incorrect because the relevant API Gateway action that signals a new deployment is CreateDeployment, not UpdateStage; reacting to UpdateStage would not reliably correspond to the deployment that produces the new SDK. Options A and B are incorrect because they rely on a CodePipeline action immediately after the deploy stage, and while plausible, the answer set treats the EventBridge-driven approach as the solution; additionally, B references a nonexistent native CodePipeline integration to export the API Gateway SDK and a CodePipeline-S3 integration to invalidate cache, neither of which performs the required SDK export and CloudFront/S3 cache refresh.

166.text:
Option C is correct because during a CodeDeploy in-place deployment behind an Application Load Balancer, the AllowTraffic lifecycle event is where instances are re-registered with the ALB target group and must pass the target group's health checks before traffic is allowed. If those health checks are misconfigured (wrong path, port, or thresholds), the instances never become healthy, so the AllowTraffic event fails. Because this is an ELB health-check failure rather than a script error, no failure reason appears in the CodeDeploy deployment logs, matching the described symptom.

Option A is incorrect because AllowTraffic is a CodeDeploy-managed event that does not run a customer script from the appspec.yml, so an "invalid script" in that hook is not the cause. Option B is incorrect because insufficient ALB permissions would surface as a clear permissions/authorization error rather than a silent AllowTraffic failure. Option D is incorrect because a missing CodeDeploy agent would cause failures much earlier in the deployment lifecycle (the instance could not run any events), not specifically at AllowTraffic.

167.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EC2 Image Builder automates building and distributing hardened AMIs, and it can publish the resulting AMI IDs to AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store. CloudFormation natively supports SSM parameter types (for example, AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<AWS::EC2::Image::Id>), so developers simply reference the parameter and always receive the latest approved AMI at stack deployment time. This removes the manual email lookup entirely and scales across all teams with a single source of truth.

Option A is incorrect because storing AMI ARNs in an encrypted S3 object and loading them via cross-stack references is convoluted; cross-stack references work with exported stack outputs, not arbitrary S3 objects, and this adds needless complexity. Option B is similarly incorrect because it routes AMI IDs through an S3 object and cross-stack references, which is far less direct and scalable than the native SSM parameter integration. Option D is incorrect because it reintroduces manual, per-team work, requiring each team to react to SNS notifications and write Lambda functions to update their stacks, defeating the goal of automation.

168.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon CodeGuru Reviewer includes a secrets detector that scans code in an associated repository (such as AWS CodeCommit) and flags hardcoded credentials. When it detects a secret, it offers a recommendation to protect it, helping migrate the value into AWS Secrets Manager. Updating the SAM templates and Python code to retrieve the database credentials from Secrets Manager is the most secure outcome because Secrets Manager supports encryption, fine-grained access control, and automatic rotation of database secrets, eliminating the hardcoded passwords.

Options A and C are incorrect because Amazon CodeGuru Profiler analyzes application runtime performance and is not a secrets-detection tool, so decorating handlers with a profiler will never find hardcoded credentials. Option D is partially right in using CodeGuru Reviewer but is less secure because it writes the secret to Systems Manager Parameter Store as a plain string rather than an encrypted secure string, and Parameter Store standard strings lack the automatic rotation and encryption that Secrets Manager provides for database credentials.

169.text:
Option B is correct because deploying API Gateway and Lambda through AWS CloudFormation with Lambda function versions gives you immutable, versioned function snapshots, and API Gateway's native canary release lets you shift a configurable percentage of production traffic to the new version while the bulk continues hitting the stable version. This lets the company test new features on a small subset of users before promoting the canary to take 100% of traffic, directly replacing the old Route 53 weighted-routing pattern with a serverless-native equivalent and minimal operational effort.

Option A is incorrect because a Route 53 failover routing policy is for active/passive disaster recovery, not for splitting a small percentage of live traffic for feature testing, so it cannot perform a true canary. Option C is incorrect because Elastic Beanstalk is designed for deploying web applications onto provisioned environments (EC2, containers) and does not deploy or manage API Gateway and Lambda as the question requires. Option D is incorrect because AWS OpsWorks is a configuration-management service built around Chef/Puppet and EC2-based layers; it does not provision API Gateway or Lambda functions and adds significant unnecessary overhead.

170.text:
Option D is correct because building one Docker image per application and deploying to Amazon ECS on AWS Fargate normalizes the heterogeneous language and framework stacks into uniform containers, eliminating differences between operating systems and servers. A separate CodeCommit repository per application gives centralized source control while preserving team boundaries, CodeBuild produces the images into Amazon ECR, and Fargate is serverless, so the company performs no patching, scaling, or OS maintenance on the underlying infrastructure. This delivers the consistent, automated pipeline with the fewest infrastructure maintenance tasks.

Option A is incorrect because forcing every application into a single repository and merging branches is unworkable for independent apps in different languages, and deploying to one centralized server creates a fragile single point of failure. Option B improves source separation but still deploys to a single centralized application server, which does not reduce maintenance or provide isolation. Option C is incorrect because baking AMIs and managing EC2 fleets with CloudFormation StackSets keeps you responsible for OS patching and instance lifecycle, which is far more maintenance than the managed, serverless Fargate approach.

171.text:
Option A is correct because sending VPC flow logs of accepted traffic to a CloudWatch Logs log group and applying a metric filter that matches the deny-list IP addresses lets you create a CloudWatch alarm that fires the moment matching traffic appears. With a 5-minute period and datapoints-to-alarm of 1, the alarm triggers in near real time on a single matching record and publishes to an Amazon SNS topic to notify the security team. This is the most cost-effective design because it uses only CloudWatch Logs, a metric filter, an alarm, and SNS, with no extra storage, query engines, or analytics services.

Option B is incorrect because Athena and QuickSight dashboards are for ad hoc analysis and visualization, not near-real-time alerting, and QuickSight adds recurring subscription cost. Option C is incorrect because standing up and running an OpenSearch Service cluster with a Lambda ingestion pipeline is expensive and operationally heavy for simple deny-list matching. Option D is incorrect because periodic Athena queries via the CloudWatch connector plus S3 event notifications introduce query latency and added cost, and they do not provide the near-real-time detection that the metric filter and alarm give.

172.text:
Option B is correct because latency-based routing in Amazon Route 53 directs each user to the API Gateway endpoint in the Region that gives the lowest latency, ensuring fast response times for North American and European users, while attached health checks fail traffic away from an unhealthy Region for high reliability. Each Region's API Gateway invokes a Lambda function in the same Region, and a DynamoDB global table provides active-active, multi-Region replication so both Regions read and write low-latency local copies of the data that stay in sync. This satisfies both the performance and the reliability requirements.

Option A is incorrect because it omits latency-based routing and uses a Region-local DynamoDB table without global replication, so data is not shared across Regions and routing is not optimized for latency. Option C is incorrect because a manual disaster-recovery API with a custom Lambda health-check that rewrites Route 53 is an active/passive design that adds failover delay and complexity rather than serving both Regions actively. Option D is incorrect because it deploys API Gateway in only North America with a single DynamoDB table, so European users get higher latency and there is no multi-Region resilience.

173.text:
Options B and D are correct because an AccessDenied error when an EC2 instance retrieves an S3 object is fundamentally an authorization failure. An error in the S3 bucket policy, such as an explicit Deny or a missing Allow for the requesting principal, blocks the download, and an error in the IAM role attached to the instance, such as missing s3:GetObject permission or a misconfigured trust or instance profile, prevents the request from being authorized. Both control whether the GetObject call is permitted, so either can produce AccessDenied.

Option A is incorrect because S3 default encryption (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS) is applied transparently on read and does not by itself deny access; a key-policy problem would surface differently and is not described here. Option C is incorrect because an object archived to S3 Glacier returns an InvalidObjectState error indicating it must be restored, not AccessDenied. Option E is incorrect because enabling S3 Versioning only tracks object versions and has no effect on access permissions, so it cannot cause an authorization error.
